cataloging_subject.label: Melusine
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous late-fifteenth-century Middle English prose translation and adaptation of Jean d’Arras’s French prose Mélusine, preserved continuously in British Library Royal MS 18 B II and printed in full by A. K. Donald in 1895. It is distinct from Jean d’Arras’s French work, Coudrette’s verse Roman de Melusine and its Middle English Romans of Partenay translation, the uncertain c. 1510 printed English fragments, Royal MS 18 B II as a physical witness, Donald’s ‘Part I’ publication package, the 1981 Kraus reprint, the University of Michigan encoding, and repository XML representations.

catalog_summary.title: Melusine
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalog_summary.date: After August 1478; Royal MS 18 B II probably copied in the 1480s

notes: The manuscript has no demonstrated original authorial title; this concise discovery title is preferred without importing Donald’s publication-part label.
This distinguishes the anonymous English prose version from Jean d’Arras’s French work and Coudrette’s distinct verse tradition.

titles.supplied_by: A. K. Donald’s 1895 edition and its 1981 reprint

scope.description: Title of the only published Donald volume, which contains the complete romance text

notes: ‘Part I’ describes an unfinished publication plan, not an incomplete medieval narrative: the published volume contains the complete romance, notes, glossary, and indexes, while the projected additional critical apparatus never appeared.

titles.value: A Chronicle of Melusine in olde Englishe. compyled by Ihon of Arras, and dedicated to the Duke of Berry and Auuergne, and translated (as yt shoulde seeme) out of Frenche into Englishe
titles.supplied_by: a later manuscript hand, dated by Donald and the British Library to the seventeenth century
notes: This is later witness paratext, not an original medieval title or proof that Jean d’Arras authored the Middle English wording.

titles.value: Here fynyssheth the noble hystorye of Melusyne

agents.name: Jean d’Arras
notes: Author of the late-fourteenth-century French prose source work; not the author of the Middle English wording.

agents.name: Alexander Karley Donald
notes: Editor and transcriber of the 1895 EETS edition.

scope.description: Responsibility for the Middle English expression cataloged here

notes: The inherited U-M author heading for Jean d’Arras identifies the French source author and must not be treated as authorship of the English translation.

scope.description: Translator-added estates list and abridged concluding paragraphs within an otherwise close prose translation

assertion.notes: The British Library catalog identifies the f. 3v estates list as inserted by the translator and the concluding ff. 217v–219v as abridged from the French original.

scope.description: Authorship of the distinct French prose source work translated into Middle English

notes: Source-work authorship is recorded separately from responsibility for the cataloged Middle English expression.

scope.description: The 1895 EETS source edition and its 1981 reprint
The edited Middle English text transcribed from Royal MS 18 B II

date_statements.display: After August 1478; exact translation date unknown

assertion.notes: The Steinschaber-family source gives a terminus after August 1478. Colquitt’s paper evidence points to an 1480s context for the surviving Royal manuscript, but manuscript copying does not by itself prove the exact translation date; the normalized upper bound follows the current British Library witness range.

date_statements.display: Royal MS 18 B II, c. 1470–1498; probably the 1480s
notes: The current catalog’s broad c. 1470–1498 range and Colquitt’s narrower 1480s paper-context argument are preserved together rather than silently choosing one.

places.label: Poitou
notes: Principal dynastic setting of the Lusignan narrative; not the proven place of English translation.
The Royal witness originated in England and scholarship identifies an English translation. Colquitt’s London paper-circulation context is witness-level evidence, not a proven translation location.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: French quotations in Donald’s retained notes are edition-layer commentary, not a second language of the medieval English work.

form_statements.description: Continuous prose romance divided in Donald’s edition and the CME encoding into a proem and sixty-two numbered chapters.

subject_statements.label: Representations of Christian-Saracen conflict

scope.description: Eastern Mediterranean campaigns and battles involving Melusine’s descendants

notes: The label records the romance’s polemical narrative content and does not endorse its representations.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose translation of Jean d’Arras’s Mélusine traces the fairy Melusine’s conditional marriage to Raymondin, her building of Lusignan and establishment of a prodigious dynasty, and the chivalric and crusading careers of their sons across Europe and the eastern Mediterranean. Raymondin’s breach of the prohibition against seeing Melusine on Saturdays exposes her serpentine transformation and separates the couple; the romance continues through family catastrophe, penance, dynastic succession, marvels, combat, and the adventures of Melusine’s sisters and descendants.

Royal MS 18 B II preserves the complete continuous English prose romance at ff. 1r–219v. Donald’s 1895 volume labels itself ‘Part I’ because an intended further apparatus volume was never published, but pp. 1–371 contain the full romance; pp. 373–408 contain Donald’s notes, glossary, and indexes. The CME XML encodes the romance text on printed pp. 1–371 and six retained notes, while omitting the title page, table of contents, most notes, glossary, and indexes.

Chapter placement and variant evidence associate the English translation with the text of Adam Steinschaber’s August 1478 Geneva print or an incunable extremely close to it. This is a text-family conclusion, not identification of the anonymous translator’s physical exemplar. The Mazarine Inc 1284 copy cached for comparison is an incomplete extant copy of the 1478 edition, not the proven source book.

extent.description: One complete prose romance represented in the pinned XML by one proem and sixty-two numbered chapter DIV1 elements (63 DIV1 total), 266 BODY paragraphs, 64 BODY headings, 371 page breaks spanning Donald pp. 1–371, 435 manuscript-folio milestones from 1b through 219b, and six retained NOTE1 elements. The first manuscript recto is represented before the first 1b milestone. This extent counts the work once; nested prologue, estates list, and chapter aggregate are not additional works.

content_parts.label: Complete Middle English prose Melusine
content_parts.summary: The complete anonymous English prose translation from its prologue through Jean d’Arras’s concluding first-person statement and the Melusyne colophon.

content_parts.label: Prologue
content_parts.summary: The translated prologue invokes the Creator, identifies Jean d’Arras’s patrons and claimed chronicle sources, and dates the French author’s beginning of the work to the Wednesday associated with St Clement’s day in 1387.
notes: The long heading displayed before the prologue was added to the manuscript in the seventeenth century and is not counted as an original title.

content_parts.label: List of the estates of Melusine’s sons
content_parts.summary: A genealogical list naming the realms and lordships held by Melusine and Raymondin’s sons, identified by the British Library catalog as an insertion by the English translator.

locators.value: within DIV1.2 before the main Elynas narrative

content_parts.label: Romance narrative in sixty-two chapters
content_parts.summary: The translated romance from its opening meditation on marvels and Melusine’s ancestry through the Lusignan dynasty, eastern campaigns, Melusine’s departure, Geffray’s penance and adventures, the Armenian king’s failed test at Melior’s sparrowhawk castle, and the concluding authorial statement and colophon.

locators.value: ff. 3v–219v; the romance proper is cataloged from f. 3v, with the principal narrative ending f. 217v and abridged concluding paragraphs through f. 219v

content_parts.label: Six Donald notes retained in the CME representation
content_parts.summary: Edition-layer notes on the later heading, a manuscript price annotation, an omission and chapter divisions relative to French versions, a cross-reference, and the disputed concluding date in Brunet and Harley MS 4418.

locators.value: six BODY NOTE1 elements, IDs note.1–note.6

notes: These notes are part of the represented catalog item but not of the medieval work. The XML header’s claim that only corrections of printing mistakes were retained does not fully describe the six notes actually present.

related_works.title: Mélusine, ou la noble histoire de Lusignan
notes: The relation is to Jean d’Arras’s French prose work. Scholarship places the English translation within the text family of Steinschaber’s 1478 print or a very close incunable descendant, without identifying a surviving physical exemplar.

related_works.title: The Romans of Partenay
notes: This is a distinct anonymous Middle English verse translation of Coudrette’s separate French verse redaction, not another witness of the prose translation cataloged here.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML for Melusine.
evidence.locator: HEADER, BODY, DIV1.1–DIV1.63, PB 1–371, and six NOTE1 elements
evidence.summary: Supplies the local title and responsibility headings, exact immediate source statement, encoded work text, chapter structure, page and folio markers, retained notes, and legacy availability statement.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped XML structure and text-boundary inspection for Melusine.xml.
evidence.locator: XPath counts and comparison with the 1895 scan
evidence.summary: Counts one proem and sixty-two chapter DIV1 elements, 266 BODY paragraphs, 64 BODY headings, 371 PB, 435 folio milestones, and six retained NOTE1 elements. The encoded print range is pp. 1–371, while inspection of the complete Donald scan identifies omitted notes, glossary, and indexes on pp. 373–408.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, Melusine item record.
evidence.locator: Item metadata, contents list, and rights statement
evidence.summary: Identifies Melusine. Part I, Donald as editor, the 1981 Kraus publication, the complete proem-plus-sixty-two-chapter contents, and the current U-M public-domain/No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: A. K. Donald, ed., Melusine, Part I: Text, Notes, and Glossary, EETS Extra Series 68 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1895), complete University of Toronto scan.
evidence.locator: title and contents; romance pp. 1–371; Notes and Illustrations pp. 373–388; Glossary pp. 389–400; indexes pp. 401–408
evidence.summary: Provides the complete 1895 edition and confirms that its sole published ‘Part I’ contains the entire romance text followed by Donald’s notes, glossary, and indexes.

evidence.citation: The Online Books Page, record for Donald’s Melusine.
evidence.summary: Explicitly notes that ‘Part I’ was the only part published and includes the full text, supporting separation of the complete romance from the unfinished publication plan.

evidence.citation: Early English Text Society publication prospectus, preliminary advertisement bound with the 1898 reprint of The Romance of the Cheuelere Assigne, PDF p. 3.
evidence.locator: PDF page 3, EETS plans for Extra-Series texts
evidence.summary: Describes the projected second part of Melusine as an introduction with ten facsimiles of foreign black-letter woodblocks, a glossary, and related apparatus, confirming that the unpublished continuation was not additional romance narrative.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Royal MS 18 B II, Romance of Melusine, record 040-002107496.
evidence.locator: scope and content, date range, physical description, provenance, related-material transcription, and access
evidence.summary: Identifies the English prose translation and its folio structure, translator-added estates list, complete concluding range, c. 1470–1498 date, English origin, codicology, later heading, and current consultation conditions. It also distinguishes the narrative ending on f. 217v from abridged concluding paragraphs through f. 219v.

evidence.citation: Olivia Colquitt, ‘The Paper of London, British Library, Royal Manuscript 18 B II,’ Notes and Queries 70.1 (2023): 7–11, accepted manuscript.
evidence.locator: pp. 7–11; accepted-manuscript discussion of source family, codex, and four watermarks
evidence.summary: Describes Royal MS 18 B II as the earliest and most complete surviving continuous witness, confirms the Steinschaber-or-close-incunable source hypothesis, and argues from four paper stocks used by Caxton and de Machlinia in 1483–1487 for an 1480s manuscript context without proving a Caxton connection.

evidence.citation: Lydia Zeldenrust, ‘The Fragments of a Middle English Melusyne Edition: Some Further Clues,’ Journal of the Early Book Society 20 (2017): 251–264.
evidence.summary: Documents the six Bodleian printed fragments, questions an exact 1510 date while supporting Wynkyn de Worde as likely printer, preserves competing abridgment versus common-exemplar hypotheses, and explains why Steinschaber’s text or a very close incunable—not necessarily a physical 1478 copy—is the likely French source family.

evidence.citation: Bibliothèque Mazarine Inc 1284, Jean d’Arras, Mélusine (Geneva: Adam Steinschaber, August 1478), Institut de France digital object.
evidence.locator: complete 401-view digitization of the incomplete Mazarine copy; catalog describes 160 leaves
evidence.summary: Identifies and reproduces the August 1478 Geneva print represented by Mazarine Inc 1284. Scholarship identifies the physical copy as incomplete; it is a comparison representative of the source text family, not the translator’s proven exemplar.

evidence.citation: Jean d’Arras, Mélusine, nouvelle édition conforme à celle de 1478, revue et corrigée, with preface by Charles Brunet (Paris: P. Jannet, 1854).
evidence.locator: title page, preface, and French text; Donald cites p. 420
evidence.summary: Confirms Brunet’s relationship to the 1478 edition and supplies the French edition Donald repeatedly cites for comparison and the disputed concluding date.

evidence.citation: General Corpus work metadata manifest for CME00045, The Romans of Partenay.
evidence.locator: cataloging subject, related works, and work boundary
evidence.summary: Establishes the project’s existing distinction between the Coudrette-derived Middle English verse romance and the Jean d’Arras-derived prose Melusine cataloged here.

open_questions.question: Which physical French incunable, if any extant copy, did the anonymous Middle English translator use?
next_steps: Collate Royal MS 18 B II against every surviving Steinschaber-family incunable, including later editions that reproduce the 1478 text closely.
Treat the cached incomplete Mazarine copy as a family representative and do not promote it to a direct exemplar without discriminating variant evidence.

open_questions.question: Are the c. 1510 Bodleian Melusyne fragments an abridgment descended from the Royal prose version, a witness to a shared English exemplar, or a separate translation from a common French source?
next_steps: Collate all six Bodleian fragments and the two reported Bandinel leaves against Royal MS 18 B II and the relevant French incunabula.
Preserve Nolan’s abridgment hypothesis and Meale’s common-exemplar alternatives until the fragmentary evidence permits a firmer stemma.

open_questions.question: Can the translation itself be dated or localized more narrowly than after August 1478 in late-fifteenth-century England?
next_steps: Separate linguistic dating of the translation from codicological dating of Royal MS 18 B II.
Compare the translator’s dialect and lexical habits with securely localized late-fifteenth-century prose while retaining Colquitt’s London paper evidence only at witness level.

notes: The U-M and Donald author labels name Jean d’Arras because he authored the French source; this manifest assigns the anonymous English expression to an anonymous Middle English translator.

Royal MS 18 B II is the earliest and most complete continuous witness, but the survival of printed English fragments means Donald’s historical ‘unique manuscript’ wording should not be expanded into a claim that no other English textual evidence survives.

The current Royal catalog’s c. 1470–1498 range and Colquitt’s probable 1480s context supersede an unqualified repetition of Donald’s ‘englisht about 1500,’ while the older date remains historically important.
The work is complete in Donald’s published Part I; the absent projected continuation concerns publication apparatus, not missing romance narrative.
